title: Corruption-robust Offline Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning From Human Feedback
abstract: We consider robustness against data corruption in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning from human feedback (MARLHF) under a strong-contamination model: given a dataset $D$ of trajectory-preference tuples (each preference being an $n$-dimensional binary label vector representing each of the $n$ agents' preferences), an $ε$-fraction of the samples may be arbitrarily corrupted. We model the problem using the framework of linear Markov games. First, under a uniform coverage assumption - where every policy of interest is sufficiently represented in the clean (prior to corruption) data - we introduce a robust estimator that guarantees an $O(ε^{1 - o(1)})$ bound on the Nash equilibrium gap. Next, we move to the more challenging unilateral coverage setting, in which only a Nash equilibrium and its single-player deviations are covered. In this case, our proposed algorithm achieves an $O(\sqrtε)$ bound on the Nash gap. Both of these procedures, however, suffer from intractable computation. To address this, we relax our solution concept to coarse correlated equilibria (CCE). Under the same unilateral coverage regime, we derive a quasi-polynomial-time algorithm whose CCE gap scales as $O(\sqrtε)$. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic treatment of adversarial data corruption in offline MARLHF.

sections.heading: 1INTRODUCTION
sections.text:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) (Christiano et al. , 2017 ) has surged in popularity as a straightforward, efficient means of fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) (Ouyang and others, 2022 ) . While most existing work focuses on single-agent settings, many real-world applications involve multiple interacting decision-makers, such as autonomous vehicles, distributed systems, or strategic marketplaces. Extending RLHF to such settings leads to multi-agent RLHF (MARLHF), where the goal is to learn aligned joint policies from preference data. Yet, despite its importance, research on MARLHF remains remarkably limited (Zhang et al. , 2024 ) .

At the same time, a critical shortcoming of all learning algorithms is their susceptibility to data-poisoning attacks—and RLHF is no exception (Wang et al. , 2023a ; Shi et al. , 2023 ; Rando and Tramèr, 2023 ; Nika et al. , 2025 ; Baumgärtner et al. , 2024 ) . By injecting malicious feedback or subtly corrupting preference labels, adversaries can steer LLMs toward biased or harmful outputs—an especially serious risk as these models are increasingly deployed in safety-critical settings. Mandal et al. ( 2025 ) have proposed robust methods against data corruption for single-agent RLHF. However, no prior work has tackled the robustness of MARLHF systems against data‐poisoning attacks. The added strategic complexity of MARLHF can amplify the impact of such attacks. It is therefore unclear whether single-agent methods extend directly to this setting.

Motivated by the above, we initiate the study of corruption-robust offline MARLHF. Our setting assumes access to preference data D = { ( τ , τ ′ , o ) } D=\{(\tau,\tau^{\prime},o)\} of size m m , where τ , τ ′ \tau,\tau^{\prime} are two sample trajectories and o o is an n n -dimensional vector of binary entries, each denoting a preference over the trajectory pair, corresponding to one of n n agents. We assume that an ϵ \epsilon -fraction of D D is arbitrarily corrupted by an attacker. Using the standard Bradley-Terry (BT) (Bradley and Terry, 1952 ) preference model, we cast our problem as an instance of a linear Markov game. Previous work has already established that the optimal theoretical guarantees in corruption‐robust offline RL and two‐player zero‐sum Markov games, under linear function approximation, exhibit a linear dependence on ϵ \epsilon . However, such dependence is achieved only when the data covers all possible directions (i.e. uniform coverage ). When the data covers only a Nash policy and its unilateral deviations (i.e. unilateral coverage ), prior work in RLHF (also RL and two-player zero-sum MGs) achieves ϵ \sqrt{\epsilon} bounds. An immediate question is whether we can attain the same ϵ \epsilon -dependent robustness rates in MARLHF as those of RLHF. Optimal dependence can be expected under uniform coverage. However, weaker coverage assumptions (e.g. unilateral coverage) imply a more challenging setting.

sections.heading: 2PRELIMINARIES
sections.text: This section contains the background technical material to be used throughout the paper.

sections.heading: 2.1Markov Games
sections.text: A Markov game of finite horizon H H between n n agents is defined by the tuple G = ( S , { A i } i = 1 n , { P h } h = 0 H − 1 , { ℛ i , h } h = 0 H − 1 , s 0 ) , G=\bigl(S,\{A_{i}\}_{i=1}^{n},\{P_{h}\}^{H-1}_{h=0},\{\mathcal{R}_{i,h}\}^{H-1}_{h=0},s_{0}\bigr), where S S is the state space, A i A_{i} is the action set of agent i i , P h : S × A 1 × ⋯ × A n → Δ ​ ( S ) P_{h}:S\times A_{1}\times\cdots\times A_{n}\to\Delta(S) is the state transition kernel at time-step h h ; the map ℛ i , h : S × A 1 × ⋯ × A n → Δ ​ ( ℝ ) \mathcal{R}_{i,h}:S\times A_{1}\times\cdots\times A_{n}\to\Delta(\mathbb{R}) denotes the random reward of agent i i at time-step h h , with R i , h ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) := 𝔼 ​ [ ℛ i , h ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) | s , a ] R_{i,h}(s,\bm{a}):=\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{R}_{i,h}(s,\bm{a})|s,a] ; finally, s 0 ∈ S s_{0}\in S denotes the initial state.

sections.heading: 2.2Preference Data
sections.text: Following the formulation in (Zhang et al. , 2024 ) , we denote by D ~ = { ( τ ~ i , τ ~ i ′ , o ~ i ) } i = 1 m \widetilde{D}=\left\{(\widetilde{\tau}_{i},\widetilde{\tau}^{\prime}_{i},\widetilde{o}_{i})\right\}^{m}_{i=1} the clean preference dataset, where τ ~ = ( s ~ 0 , a ~ 1 , 0 , a ~ 2 , 0 , … , a ~ n , 0 , s ~ 1 , … , s ~ H − 1 ) \widetilde{\tau}=(\widetilde{s}_{0},\widetilde{a}_{1,0},\widetilde{a}_{2,0},\ldots,\widetilde{a}_{n,0},\widetilde{s}_{1},\ldots,\widetilde{s}_{H-1}) and τ ~ ′ = ( s ~ 0 ′ , a ~ 1 , 0 ′ , a ~ 2 , 0 ′ , … , a ~ n , 0 ′ , s ~ 1 ′ , … , s ~ H − 1 ′ ) \widetilde{\tau}^{\prime}=(\widetilde{s}^{\prime}_{0},\widetilde{a}^{\prime}_{1,0},\widetilde{a}^{\prime}_{2,0},\ldots,\widetilde{a}^{\prime}_{n,0},\widetilde{s}^{\prime}_{1},\ldots,\widetilde{s}^{\prime}_{H-1}) denote sampled trajectories from behavior policies 𝝁 \bm{\mu} and 𝝁 ref \bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}} , respectively, and o ~ i = ( o ~ i , 1 , … , o ~ i , n ) \widetilde{o}_{i}=(\widetilde{o}_{i,1},\ldots,\widetilde{o}_{i,n}) , with o ~ i , j ∈ { − 1 , + 1 } \widetilde{o}_{i,j}\in\{-1,+1\} , for all j ∈ [ n ] j\in[n] , gives information about individual preferences of agents for each pair of trajectories: o ~ i , j = 1 \widetilde{o}_{i,j}=1 implies that τ ~ i \widetilde{\tau}_{i} is preferred to τ ~ i ′ \widetilde{\tau}^{\prime}_{i} for agent j j . We assume that the preferences are generated according to the Bradley-Terry (BT) model (Bradley and Terry, 1952 ) : for each agent j j , we assume that we have ℙ ​ ( o ~ i , j = 1 | τ ~ i , τ ~ i ′ ) \displaystyle\mathbb{P}(\widetilde{o}_{i,j}=1|\widetilde{\tau}_{i},\widetilde{\tau}^{\prime}_{i}) = σ ​ ( ∑ h = 0 H − 1 R i , h ​ ( s ~ h , 𝒂 ~ h ) − ∑ h = 0 H − 1 R i , h ​ ( s ~ h ′ , 𝒂 ~ h ′ ) ) , \displaystyle=\sigma\left(\sum^{H-1}_{h=0}R_{i,h}(\widetilde{s}_{h},\widetilde{\bm{a}}_{h})-\sum^{H-1}_{h=0}R_{i,h}(\widetilde{s}^{\prime}_{h},\widetilde{\bm{a}}^{\prime}_{h})\right)~, with σ ​ ( x ) = 1 / ( 1 + exp ⁡ ( − x ) ) \sigma(x)=1/(1+\exp(-x)) being the sigmoid function.

sections.heading: 2.3Corruption Model
sections.text: Following the ϵ \epsilon -corruption model in offline RL(HF) (Zhang et al. , 2022 ; Mandal et al. , 2025 ) , we assume that there exists an attacker that has full access to the dataset D ~ \widetilde{D} and arbitrarily perturbs an ϵ \epsilon -fraction of it. That is, given ϵ ∈ [ 0 , 1 / 2 ) \epsilon\in[0,1/2) , we assume that the attacker inspects D ~ \widetilde{D} and modifies up to ϵ ⋅ m \epsilon\cdot m samples in D ~ \widetilde{D} . We denote by D D the poisoned dataset. In other words, there are at most ϵ ⋅ m \epsilon\cdot m data samples in D D such that ( τ , τ ′ , o ) ≠ ( τ ~ , τ ~ ′ , o ~ ) (\tau,\tau^{\prime},o)\neq(\widetilde{\tau},\widetilde{\tau}^{\prime},\widetilde{o}) . 1 1 1 Following prior literature on corruption-robust RL (Zhang et al. , 2022 ) , we assume that, for each h ∈ [ H − 1 ] h\in[H-1] , the subset of D D containing only the h h steps of the samples, is also consistent with the ϵ \epsilon -corruption model.

sections.heading: 2.4Data Coverage
sections.text: Offline learning problems necessitate access to a dataset that contains, at least to some extent, "good" samples, in the sense that they are traversed by policies we are trying to approximate. This condition is usually described by the notion of data coverage . In linear Markov games (MG), data coverage is captured by the feature covariance matrix. Formally, for every h ∈ [ H − 1 ] h\in[H-1] , we define Σ 𝝁 ​ ( h ) = 𝔼 𝝁 h ​ [ ϕ ​ ( s h , 𝒂 h ) ​ ϕ ​ ( s h , 𝒂 h ) ⊤ ] \Sigma_{\bm{\mu}}(h)=\mathbb{E}_{\bm{\mu}_{h}}\left[\phi(s_{h},\bm{a}_{h})\phi(s_{h},\bm{a}_{h})^{\top}\right] and Σ 𝝁 , 𝝁 ref − = 𝔼 𝝁 , 𝝁 ref ​ [ ( ϕ ​ ( τ ) − ϕ ​ ( τ ′ ) ) ​ ( ϕ ​ ( τ ) − ϕ ​ ( τ ′ ) ) ⊤ ] \Sigma^{-}_{\bm{\mu},\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}=\mathbb{E}_{{\bm{\mu}},{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}}\left[\left(\phi(\tau)-\phi(\tau^{\prime})\right)\left(\phi(\tau)-\phi(\tau^{\prime})\right)^{\top}\right] as the feature covariance matrices that will determine coverage of our given data. Here ϕ ​ ( τ ) = ∑ h = 0 H − 1 ϕ ​ ( s h , 𝒂 h ) \phi(\tau)=\sum^{H-1}_{h=0}\phi(s_{h},\bm{a}_{h}) . Note that Σ 𝝁 ​ ( h ) \Sigma_{\bm{\mu}}(h) is the standard covariance matrix used in the literature of offline RL, while Σ 𝝁 , 𝝁 ref − \Sigma^{-}_{\bm{\mu},\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}} is the difference covariance matrix which has been previously used in RLHF literature (Zhan et al. , 2023 ) .

sections.heading: 3ROBUST NE LEARNING UNDER UNIFORM COVERAGE
sections.text:
We start by considering the case when the preference dataset has uniform coverage, that is, all basis directions of the feature space are sufficiently covered. We state this assumption below.

On a high level, all our algorithms are based on the following pipeline. They first use the preference data to robustly estimate each agent’s reward parameters. Then, using those rewards, they proceed to compute robust pessimistic and optimistic estimates of the individual Q Q -functions for policies of interest. Finally, they estimate the gap and output a joint policy that minimizes it. We will instantiate different versions of this pipeline under different coverage assumptions and notions of equilibria. Robust Reward Estimation ⇒ Robust Q-function Estimation \displaystyle\quad\quad\Rightarrow\textnormal{Robust Q-function Estimation} ⇒ Estimated Gap Minimization \displaystyle\quad\quad\quad\quad\Rightarrow\textnormal{Estimated Gap Minimization}

sections.heading: 3.1Algorithm
sections.text:
The main idea of our proposed algorithm is as follows. First, note that our overall objective is to find a joint policy that minimizes the Nash gap with respect to the ground-truth reward functions. However, we have access neither to these functions, nor to the real environment. We are only given a finite preference dataset D D , an ϵ \epsilon -fraction of which is arbitrarily corrupted. Therefore, our first step is to compute robust estimates of the reward parameters of each agent. Note that, for linear rewards, maximum likelihood estimation becomes standard logistic regression. And for such an objective, it is known (Awasthi et al. , 2022 ) that we can recover the true parameter of interest from ϵ \epsilon -corrupted data with O ​ ( ϵ 1 − o ​ ( 1 ) ) O(\epsilon^{1-o(1)}) accuracy via a robust method called TrimmedMLE (for a pseudocode, see Algorithm 6 in Appendix E ). Thus, for each agent i i , we let θ ~ i = TrimmedMLE ​ ( D , ϵ , ν ) \widetilde{\theta}_{i}=\texttt{TrimmedMLE}\left(D,\epsilon,\nu\right) ,
where we denote by θ i \theta_{i} the H ​ d Hd -dimensional result of the concatenation of θ i , h \theta_{i,h} , for h ∈ [ H − 1 ] h\in[H-1] , and ν \nu denotes a granularity hyperparameter.

Next, since we are in the offline setting, the most reasonable approach is to apply pessimism with respect to the recovered parameters. To that end, we form confidence sets, for each agent i i , based on the TrimmedMLE guarantees: Θ Unif ​ ( θ ~ i ) = { θ ∈ Θ : ‖ θ ~ i − θ ‖ 2 ≤ O ​ ( ϵ 1 − o ​ ( 1 ) ξ R ) } , \displaystyle\Theta_{\textnormal{Unif}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i})=\left\{\theta\in\Theta:\left\lVert\widetilde{\theta}_{i}-\theta\right\rVert_{2}\leq O\left(\frac{\epsilon^{1-o(1)}}{\xi_{R}}\right)\right\}~, (1) where δ > 0 \delta>0 is a randomness parameter. Once we have access to the confidence set, we compute the boundary parameters R ¯ i , h ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) \displaystyle\overline{R}_{i,h}(s,\bm{a}) = max θ i ∈ Θ Unif ​ ( θ ~ i ) ⁡ θ i , h ⊤ ​ ϕ ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) , \displaystyle=\max_{\theta_{i}\in\Theta_{\textnormal{Unif}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i})}\theta_{i,h}^{\top}\phi(s,\bm{a}), (2) R ¯ i , h ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) \displaystyle\underline{R}_{i,h}(s,\bm{a}) = min θ i ∈ Θ Unif ​ ( θ ~ i ) ⁡ θ i , h ⊤ ​ ϕ ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) . \displaystyle=\min_{\theta_{i}\in\Theta_{\textnormal{Unif}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i})}\theta_{i,h}^{\top}\phi(s,\bm{a})~. (3) Note that the inner problems are convex programs that have closed-form solutions. Now that we have access to our estimate reward functions, we need to minimize the Nash gap with respect to these rewards. In order to do that, we apply backward induction. First, we initialize the value function estimates V ¯ H 𝝅 ​ ( ⋅ ) = V ¯ H † , 𝝅 − i ​ ( ⋅ ) = 0 \underline{V}^{\bm{\pi}}_{H}(\cdot)=\overline{V}^{\dagger,\bm{\pi}_{-i}}_{H}(\cdot)=0 , for every joint policy 𝝅 \bm{\pi} . Then, for every step h h down to 0 , we apply a robust estimation algorithm Rob-Q (see Algorithm 2 ) for the Q-values. Essentially, the procedure first robustly estimates the parameters of the Bellman operator using a RobEst oracle which is guaranteed to return an O ​ ( ϵ ) O(\epsilon) -close value parameter under uniform coverage (Zhang et al. , 2022 ) . Then, it computes the estimated Q-values by properly clipping the bonus-inflated (deflated) estimates so that they remain in [ − H ​ d , H ​ d ] [-H\sqrt{d},H\sqrt{d}] .

Once we have the Q Q -functions, the value function estimates V ¯ i , h 𝝅 ​ ( ⋅ ) \underline{V}^{\bm{\pi}}_{i,h}\left(\cdot\right) (and V ¯ i , h † , 𝝅 − i ​ ( ⋅ ) \overline{V}^{\dagger,\bm{\pi}_{-i}}_{i,h}\left(\cdot\right) ) for all steps, defined with respect to the estimated reward parameters, are then computed by taking expectations over (and taking max \max over actions for player i i ) the given policies. 2 2 2 Note that Algorithm 7 runs Algorithm 2 for H H steps and finally returns the estimates of the value functions. We have used Algorithm 7 to present Algorithm 1 for ease of presentation. However, Algorithm 2 will be necessary in the following sections. Once we do this for every policy, we then return the policy 𝝅 ~ \widetilde{\bm{\pi}} that minimizes the estimated gap with respect to ( R ¯ , R ¯ ) = ( R ¯ 1 , R ¯ 1 , … , R ¯ n , R ¯ n ) (\overline{R},\underline{R})=(\overline{R}_{1},\underline{R}_{1},\ldots,\overline{R}_{n},\underline{R}_{n}) : arg ⁡ min 𝝅 ⁡ Gap ~ ​ ( 𝝅 , R ¯ , R ¯ ) := ∑ i ∈ [ n ] V ¯ i , 0 † , 𝝅 − i ​ ( s 0 ) − V ¯ i , 0 𝝅 ​ ( s 0 ) . \displaystyle\arg\min_{\bm{\pi}}\widetilde{\textnormal{Gap}}\left(\bm{\pi},\overline{R},\underline{R}\right):=\sum_{i\in[n]}\overline{V}^{\dagger,\bm{\pi}_{-i}}_{i,0}\left(s_{0}\right)-\underline{V}^{\bm{\pi}}_{i,0}\left(s_{0}\right)~. (4) As shown in Appendix (see Lemma A.6 ), our optimistic and pessimistic value estimates are high-probability approximates of the true value function, which implies that the estimated gap is a high-probability upper bound on the actual Nash gap. Minimizing this surrogate gap therefore serves as a proxy for minimizing the true Nash gap—and, as the gap approaches zero, the resulting joint policy correspondingly approaches a Nash equilibrium. Algorithm 1 provides the pseudocode for the full procedure.

sections.heading: 3.2Theoretical guarantees
sections.text: In this section, we state the theoretical guarantees on the convergence of the Algorithm 1 . Proofs can be found in Appendix A .

sections.heading: 4ROBUST NE LEARNING UNDER UNILATERAL COVERAGE
sections.text:
In the previous section, we proposed an algorithm that returns an O ​ ( n ​ ϵ + n / m ) O(n\epsilon+n/\sqrt{m}) -approximate Nash equilibrium under uniform coverage. However, such coverage is rarely possible in practice. The purpose of this section is to solve the Nash gap minimization problem under a more relaxed notion of coverage, namely unilateral coverage , which simply requires coverage of a Nash policy and all its unilateral deviations for each agent. We extend the notion of low relative uncertainty of (Zhong et al. , 2022 ) to the MARLHF setting.

The first condition simply says that behavior policies 𝝁 \bm{\mu} and 𝝁 ref \bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}} sufficiently cover a Nash equilibrium and its unilateral deviations in the feature space. Different from single-agent RL, where single policy concentrability is enough to provide theoretical guarantees, its extension to Markov games, where only Nash policies are covered does not allow for any guarantees. Unilateral coverage is in fact necessary and sufficient to provide any meaningful guarantees in zero-sum (Zhong et al. , 2022 ) and general-sum Markov games (Zhang et al. , 2023 ) .

sections.heading: 4.1Algorithm
sections.text:
For the uniform coverage setting, we applied TrimmedMLE to obtain estimates for the ground-truth reward parameters. The benefit of such an approach is that, under such coverage, it comes with bounds on the ℓ 2 \ell^{2} -norm of the error, which then allows for defining our confidence set in terms of such error bounds. This, in turn, allows us to directly upper bound the difference between value functions and their estimates in terms of ℓ 2 \ell^{2} -difference of their respective reward parameters. When we do not have uniform coverage, the final estimate is not guaranteed to remain close to the true parameter in the ℓ 2 \ell^{2} sense. In this case, as shown in Appendix (see Lemma LABEL:lem:log_prob_bound_unilateral ), the parameters are close in the log-sigmoid sense. Given the output θ ~ i \widetilde{\theta}_{i} of TrimmedMLE , we define the confidence set for the unilateral coverage setting as Θ Unil ( θ ~ i ) = { θ ∈ Θ : \displaystyle\Theta_{\textnormal{Unil}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i})=\Bigg\{\theta\in\Theta: 2 m ∑ ( τ , τ ′ , o ) ∈ D log σ ​ ( o ⋅ θ ~ i ⊤ ​ ( ϕ ​ ( τ ) − ϕ ​ ( τ ′ ) ) ) σ ​ ( o ⋅ θ ⊤ ​ ( ϕ ​ ( τ ) − ϕ ​ ( τ ′ ) ) ) ≤ κ } , \displaystyle\frac{2}{m}\sum_{(\tau,\tau^{\prime},o)\in D}\log\frac{\sigma\left(o\cdot\widetilde{\theta}_{i}^{\top}\left(\phi(\tau)-\phi(\tau^{\prime})\right)\right)}{\sigma\left(o\cdot\theta^{\top}\left(\phi(\tau)-\phi(\tau^{\prime})\right)\right)}\leq\kappa\Bigg\}~, (5) where κ = 6 ​ ϵ ​ H ​ d + ( 2 ​ d / m ) ⋅ log ⁡ ( H ​ m / δ ) \kappa=6\epsilon H\sqrt{d}+(2d/m)\cdot\log(Hm/\delta) controls the ‘radius’ of the confidence set. We can provide theoretical guarantees that Θ Unil ​ ( θ ~ i ) \Theta_{\textnormal{Unil}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i}) contains the ground-truth parameter θ i ∗ \theta^{*}_{i} with high probability.

Unfortunately though, the same analysis does not go through just by choosing reward estimates that maximize (minimize) over the confidence set, due to this more complicated notion of closeness. Hence, we follow a different approach in this section. Intuitively, if we can find θ \theta in our confidence set that maximizes the gap of our output policy , and, similarly, find a policy 𝝅 \bm{\pi} that minimizes the gap with respect to this choice of θ \theta , we can finally bound the true gap on the ground-truth reward. This intuition is based on the observation that any Nash gaps of policies that used θ \theta parameters in the confidence set should be close to each-other.

Based on the above discussion, we will utilize projected gradient ascent (PGA) to update our estimates of θ \theta . However, we do not have access to the true gap given parameter θ \theta , neither of its gradient with respect to θ \theta . We thus resort to using biased estimates of it.
As we show in Appendix (see Lemma B.5 ), for any 𝜽 := ( θ 1 , … , θ n ) ∈ Θ Unil ( θ ~ i ) × … , × Θ Unil ( θ ~ n ) \bm{\theta}:=(\theta_{1},\ldots,\theta_{n})\in\Theta_{\textnormal{Unil}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{i})\times\ldots,\times\Theta_{\textnormal{Unil}}(\widetilde{\theta}_{n}) , any policy 𝝅 \bm{\pi} that is the minimizer of the estimated gap computed via Rob-Q on 𝜽 \bm{\theta} , satisfies | Gap ​ ( 𝝅 , 𝜽 ) − Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ∗ , 𝜽 ) | ≤ O ​ ( n ​ ϵ + n / m ) , |\textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi},\bm{\theta})-\textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi}^{*},\bm{\theta})|\leq O(n\sqrt{\epsilon}+n/\sqrt{m})~, for Nash equilibrium policy 𝝅 ∗ \bm{\pi}^{*} which is covered by D D , where Gap ​ ( 𝝅 , 𝜽 ) \textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi},\bm{\theta}) here denotes the true Nash gap of 𝝅 \bm{\pi} under reward function parameterized by 𝜽 \bm{\theta} .
Therefore, we optimize Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ∗ , 𝜽 ) \textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi}^{*},\bm{\theta}) as a surrogate objective, and later transfer guarantees to Gap ​ ( 𝝅 , 𝜽 ) \textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi},\bm{\theta}) using Lemma B.5 .

However, we do not have access to ∇ 𝜽 Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ∗ , 𝜽 ) \nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi}^{*},\bm{\theta}) . Here, we use the following observation: in the linear setting, the (sub)gradient of the gap becomes the average feature differences over convex combinations of occupancy measures. Thus, we can use ∇ 𝜽 ​ ∑ i ∈ [ n ] ( V i , 0 𝝁 ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) − V i , 0 𝝁 ref ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) ) \nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\sum_{i\in[n]}(V^{\bm{\mu}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})-V^{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})) as an estimate for ∇ 𝜽 Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ∗ , 𝜽 ) \nabla_{\bm{\theta}}\textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi}^{*},\bm{\theta}) , since 𝝁 \bm{\mu} and 𝝁 ref \bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}} already cover 𝝅 ∗ \bm{\pi}^{*} and its unilateral deviations. Here, V i , 0 𝝁 ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) V^{\bm{\mu}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i}) denotes the value function of 𝝁 \bm{\mu} with respect to reward function parametrized by θ i \theta_{i} . To estimate ∇ θ i ( V i , 0 𝝁 ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) − V i , 0 𝝁 ref ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) ) \nabla_{\theta_{i}}(V^{\bm{\mu}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})-V^{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})) , we use a robust mean oracle RobMean that takes as input corrupted feature differences and returns a O ​ ( ϵ ) O(\sqrt{\epsilon}) -approximate estimate of their true mean, which in our case is just ∇ θ i ( V i , 0 𝝁 ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) − V i , 0 𝝁 ref ​ ( s 0 , θ i ) ) \nabla_{\theta_{i}}(V^{\bm{\mu}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})-V^{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}_{i,0}(s_{0},\theta_{i})) . We thus define our gradient estimate ∇ ~ θ i ​ Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ∗ , 𝜽 ) \widetilde{\nabla}_{\theta_{i}}\textnormal{Gap}\left({\bm{\pi}}^{*},\bm{\theta}\right) with respect to θ i \theta_{i} as ∑ h = 0 H − 1 RobMean ​ ( D h , ϕ 𝝁 ) − ∑ h = 0 H − 1 RobMean ​ ( D h , ϕ 𝝁 ref ) , \displaystyle\sum^{H-1}_{h=0}\texttt{RobMean}\left(D^{\bm{\mu}}_{h,\phi}\right)-\sum^{H-1}_{h=0}\texttt{RobMean}\left(D^{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}_{h,\phi}\right)~, where D h , ϕ 𝝁 D^{\bm{\mu}}_{h,\phi} and D h , ϕ 𝝁 ref D^{\bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}}}_{h,\phi} partition each h h -level of D D and store only the features of trajectories generated by 𝝁 \bm{\mu} and 𝝁 ref \bm{\mu}_{\textnormal{ref}} , respectively. After running PGA for T 1 T_{1} steps, we compute the empirical average of the iterates and use that to compute our estimated reward function. The reward estimation procedure RewardEst is described in Algorithm 3 . Once we have access to this reward, we can run Rob-Q on it and obtain estimated gaps for each policy.

However, lack of uniform coverage implies weaker guarantees on Rob-Q . Thus, we need to properly define a bonus term that accounts for corruption and lack of coverage. First, let us define a scaled sample covariance matrix with respect to the preferred trajectories in the corrupted data, using regularization parameter λ ≥ 0 \lambda\geq 0 (to be specified later) as Λ h = 3 5 ​ ( 1 m ​ ∑ j = 1 m ϕ ​ ( s h j , 𝒂 h j ) ​ ϕ ​ ( s h j , 𝒂 h j ) ⊤ + ( ϵ + λ ) ​ I ) . \Lambda_{h}=\frac{3}{5}\left(\frac{1}{m}\sum^{m}_{j=1}\phi(s^{j}_{h},\bm{a}^{j}_{h})\phi(s^{j}_{h},\bm{a}^{j}_{h})^{\top}+(\epsilon+\lambda)I\right). Using Λ h \Lambda_{h} , we now define the bonus term to be used in this section as follows. For any ( s , 𝒂 ) (s,\bm{a}) , let Γ ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) = E ​ ( d , m , δ , ϵ ) ⋅ ‖ ϕ ​ ( s , 𝒂 ) ‖ Λ h − 1 , \Gamma(s,\bm{a})=E(d,m,\delta,\epsilon)\cdot\left\lVert\phi(s,\bm{a})\right\rVert_{\Lambda_{h}^{-1}}~, where E ​ ( d , m , δ , ϵ ) = O ​ ( ϵ + 1 / m ) E(d,m,\delta,\epsilon)=O(\sqrt{\epsilon}+1/\sqrt{m}) (see Appendix B for detailed definition). We run Rob-Q with bonus set as Γ \Gamma and obtain estimated gaps for every joint policy. Finally, we return a joint policy that minimizes estimated gap. The full procedure is described in Algorithm 4 .

sections.heading: 4.2Theoretical guarantees
sections.text: In this section, we state the theoretical guarantee on the convergence of Algorithm 4 .

sections.heading: 5ROBUST CCE LEARNING UNDER UNILATERAL COVERAGE
sections.text:
In the previous section, we provided an algorithm that was designed to compute an approximate NE using corrupted preference data under the minimal unilateral coverage assumption. However, a key bottleneck of Algorithm 4 is the intractability of the gap-minimization step. It is well-known that even normal-form general-sum games suffer from the curse of multi-agents—computational time scales exponentially with the number of agents (actions) (Foster et al. , 2023 ) . Thus, to address this issue, previous work has considered more relaxed versions of the NE, such as correlated equilibria or coarse correlated equilibria (CCE) (Cui et al. , 2023 ; Zhang et al. , 2023 ; Ma et al. , 2023 ; Song et al. , 2021 ) , the latter of which can be approximated using no-regret learning algorithms.

A general correlated policy is defined as a set of H H maps 𝝅 := { 𝝅 h : Ω × ( S × A ) h − 1 × S → Δ ​ ( A ) } h ∈ [ H − 1 ] \bm{\pi}:=\{\bm{\pi}_{h}:\Omega\times(S\times A)^{h-1}\times S\rightarrow\Delta(A)\}_{h\in[H-1]} , where the first argument w ∈ Ω w\in\Omega is sampled from some underlying distribution. A crucial difference from Markov policies is information about prior states that is given as input. We denote by Π GCP \Pi^{\textnormal{GCP}} the space of all general correlated policies. We denote by Π i GCP \Pi^{\textnormal{GCP}}_{i} the set of general correlated policies for agent i i . Then, policy 𝝅 ∗ \bm{\pi}^{*} is said to be an α \alpha -CCE if there exists α ≥ 0 \alpha\geq 0 , such that, for every agent i i and state s s , we have V i , 0 𝝅 ∗ ​ ( s ) ≥ V i , 0 π ′ , 𝝅 − i ∗ ​ ( s ) − α V^{\bm{\pi}^{*}}_{i,0}(s)\geq V^{\pi^{\prime},\bm{\pi}^{*}_{-i}}_{i,0}(s)-\alpha , for every π i ′ ∈ Π i GCP \pi^{\prime}_{i}\in\Pi^{\textnormal{GCP}}_{i} . If α = 0 \alpha=0 , then 𝝅 ∗ \bm{\pi}^{*} is said to be a CCE. Note that the only difference between NEs and CCEs is that an NE is restricted to be a product policy, while a CCE can be any arbitrary combination of individual policies in the joint action space simplex. Hereafter, we overload notation and use Gap ​ ( 𝝅 ) \textnormal{Gap}(\bm{\pi}) to denote the CCE gap of a joint policy 𝝅 \bm{\pi} .

There has been a lot of interest in efficiently computing approximate CCEs in Markov games using V-learning type algorithms (Jin et al. , 2021 ; Wang et al. , 2023b ; Cui et al. , 2023 ) . However, all these works consider the online setting, where the learner can explore the environment and increasingly gather more relevant data. We only have at our disposal offline corrupted preference data.

sections.heading: 5.1Algorithm
sections.text: In this section, we propose an offline-learning algorithm for computing approximate CCE in linear Markov games. First, we again assume unilateral coverage on our data (Assumption 2 ). Given preference data D D , we again run RewardEst procedure to obtain the reward estimates 𝜽 ^ \widehat{\bm{\theta}} . At this point, different from the previous section, we take another approach at the estimated gap minimization problem. First, for a given joint policy 𝝅 \bm{\pi} , agent i i , state s s , step h h , and actions a a and a † a^{\dagger} , we define the loss ℒ i s ​ ( a † , a ′ ) \mathcal{L}^{s}_{i}(a^{\dagger},a^{\prime}) for this stage as 𝔼 𝒂 − i ∼ 𝝅 − i , h ( ⋅ | s ) ​ [ Q ¯ i , h † , 𝝅 − i ​ ( s , a † , 𝒂 − i ) − Q ¯ i , h 𝝅 ​ ( s , a ′ , 𝒂 − i ) ] , \displaystyle\mathbb{E}_{\bm{a}_{-i}\sim\bm{\pi}_{-i,h}(\cdot|s)}\left[\overline{Q}^{\dagger,\bm{\pi}_{-i}}_{i,h}(s,a^{\dagger},\bm{a}_{-i})-\underline{Q}^{\bm{\pi}}_{i,h}(s,a^{\prime},\bm{a}_{-i})\right]~, where the optimistic and pessimistic Q Q -function estimates are computed using 𝜽 ^ \widehat{\bm{\theta}} . Using this loss function, we can now express the estimated gap minimization problem at stage h h as min 𝝅 h ′ ​ ∑ i ∈ [ n ] max π i , h † ⁡ 𝔼 a i † ∼ π i , h † ( ⋅ | s ) , a i ′ ∼ π i , h ′ ( ⋅ | s ) ​ [ ℒ i s ​ ( a † , a ′ ) ] . \displaystyle\min_{\bm{\pi}^{\prime}_{h}}\sum_{i\in[n]}\max_{\pi^{\dagger}_{i,h}}\mathbb{E}_{a^{\dagger}_{i}\sim\pi^{\dagger}_{i,h}(\cdot|s),a^{\prime}_{i}\sim\pi^{\prime}_{i,h}(\cdot|s)}\left[\mathcal{L}^{s}_{i}(a^{\dagger},a^{\prime})\right]~. Note that such an objective can be framed as a normal-form game at stage h h . To solve the stage game, we utilize OptimisticHedge (Daskalakis et al. , 2021 ) , a no-regret learning algorithm which returns a O ~ ​ ( 1 / T 2 ) \widetilde{O}(1/T_{2}) -approximate CCE of the game when run for T 2 T_{2} iterations. Each player basically solves a max − min \max-\min problem at stage h h and updates its policy using a multiplicative weights update style. We run the algorithm for T 2 T_{2} iterations and return the average joint policy. The pseudo-code for Optimistic Hedge applied to our setting is given in Algorithm 8 (see Appendix E ). Once we have computed our joint policy at stage h h , we then compute the optimistic and pessimistic values by taking expectations of the Q Q -function estimates over the newly computed policies. We use these value estimates to run the next iteration h − 1 h-1 of our algorithm. Finally, we return the joint policy 𝝅 ~ \widetilde{\bm{\pi}} which is a composition of the returned policies from OptimisticHedge at each stage h h . The full procedure is given in Algorithm 5 .

sections.heading: 5.2Theoretical guarantees
sections.text: In this section, we provide upper bounds on the CCE gap for the output of Algorithm 5 .

sections.heading: 7DISCUSSION
sections.text:
In this paper, we studied the problem of data corruption in offline MARLHF. We proposed provable-robust algorithms, both under uniform and unilateral coverage assumptions. Finally, we proposed a computationally efficient algorithm that robustly approximates a coarse correlated equilibrium of the underlying Markov game.

A key technical contribution of our work is a new way to optimize the Nash gap without access to true reward functions or their gradients. Prior single-agent RLHF approaches rely on primal-dual methods or unbiased gradients, which do not extend to general-sum Markov games due to strategic coupling. We instead introduce a biased but tractable gradient surrogate : by leveraging the linear structure of the underlying Markov game, we approximate the gradient at a Nash equilibrium using feature expectations induced by behavior policies. Under unilateral coverage, these policies capture the occupancy measures of the equilibrium and its unilateral deviations, so their feature differences act as a proxy for the true gradient direction. Despite the bias, this estimate is accurate enough to guide projected gradient ascent over the reward confidence set, yielding O ​ ( ϵ ) O(\sqrt{\epsilon}) robustness guarantees. This idea—leveraging equilibrium structure to construct usable gradient surrogates from corrupted offline preference data—appears to be new and may be of independent interest.

Several interesting directions are worth pursuing. First, it is not clear how one can formulate the data corruption problem in MARLHF with general function approximation, and then how to design robust algorithms in that setting. Second, it would be interesting to address the open question of whether the O ​ ( ϵ ) O(\sqrt{\epsilon}) bound under non-uniform coverage is tight. Finally, implementing the proposed algorithms and experimentally testing them on MARL environments is another exciting future direction.
